title: What is the difference between stack and heap memory?
description: Compare stack and heap memory allocation.
options: Stack: LIFO, auto-managed, fast allocation/deallocation, stores local variables and function calls, limited size. Heap: dynamic allocation (malloc/new), slower, managed manually or by GC, stores objects, larger but fragmented
correct_answer: Stack: LIFO, auto-managed, fast allocation/deallocation, stores local variables and function calls, limited size. Heap: dynamic allocation (malloc/new), slower, managed manually or by GC, stores objects, larger but fragmented

title: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control?
options: Optimistic: allow concurrent access, validate before commit, retry on conflict; works well with low contention. Pessimistic: lock resources upfront, prevent conflicts; works well with high contention. Optimistic scales better but may have retries
correct_answer: Optimistic: allow concurrent access, validate before commit, retry on conflict; works well with low contention. Pessimistic: lock resources upfront, prevent conflicts; works well with high contention. Optimistic scales better but may have retries

title: What is eventual consistency?
description: How does eventual consistency work in distributed systems?
options: After a write, replicas may return stale data temporarily, but all replicas will converge to the same value eventually (given no new writes). BASE model: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent. Contrast: strong consistency (immediate)
correct_answer: After a write, replicas may return stale data temporarily, but all replicas will converge to the same value eventually (given no new writes). BASE model: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent. Contrast: strong consistency (immediate)

title: Implement Rabin-Karp string matching with rolling hash.
correct_answer:
def rabin_karp(text, pattern, base=256, mod=101):
    n, m = len(text), len(pattern)
    if m > n: return []
    
    results = []
    p_hash = t_hash = 0
    h = pow(base, m - 1, mod)
    
    for i in range(m):
        p_hash = (base * p_hash + ord(pattern[i])) % mod
        t_hash = (base * t_hash + ord(text[i])) % mod
    
    for i in range(n - m + 1):
        if p_hash == t_hash:
            if text[i:i+m] == pattern:  # verify to avoid false positives
                results.append(i)
        if i < n - m:
            t_hash = (base * (t_hash - ord(text[i]) * h) + ord(text[i + m])) % mod
            if t_hash < 0: t_hash += mod
    
    return results

title: What is a write-ahead log (WAL)?
description: How does WAL work in databases?
options: WAL writes changes to a log file BEFORE modifying data pages; ensures durability and crash recovery. On crash, uncommitted changes are rolled back and committed changes replayed from the log. Used by PostgreSQL, SQLite, InnoDB (redo log)
correct_answer: WAL writes changes to a log file BEFORE modifying data pages; ensures durability and crash recovery. On crash, uncommitted changes are rolled back and committed changes replayed from the log. Used by PostgreSQL, SQLite, InnoDB (redo log)

title: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable delivery, ordered, flow control, congestion control; used for HTTP, SSH, databases. UDP: connectionless, unreliable, unordered, faster, lower overhead; used for DNS, video streaming, gaming, VoIP
correct_answer: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable delivery, ordered, flow control, congestion control; used for HTTP, SSH, databases. UDP: connectionless, unreliable, unordered, faster, lower overhead; used for DNS, video streaming, gaming, VoIP
